speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 21, 1995. The survivor is Harry Toporek. The interviewer is Marcia Blitstein. We're in Lincolnwood, Illinois, USA, and this is conducted in English. Again, I'm Marcia Blitstein. Today is June 21, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Mr. Harry Toporek. This is Lincolnwood, Illinois, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- When you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello. I'd like to begin this interview by thanking you for doing it for us. It's very important. And we're delighted that-- that you have agreed to do this. Could you please state your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Harry Toporek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell Toporek?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-O-P-O-R-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever used any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. No, no, no. Not whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your Jewish name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tzvi-- it's.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born April the 20th, 1923, in Lask, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 72-years-old today.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in Lask, Poland. What large cities is that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz. That was a big industrial city-- the name, Lodz. It was about 32 kilometers from our town, from Lask.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Esther. Her maiden name was Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was also born in Lask.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Moshe Toporek. And he was 58 years old in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Lask.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about Lask. Was it a-- a large community? Were there a lot of Jews in the community? Tell us something about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lask was a small city with 350 Jewish families. We were probably about 50% percent from the population before the war. Too many rich people in the city were not. But we all made a living, and somehow, we survive. I remember that my parents, I mean, that they were struggling for making a-- ends meet-- let's put it this way. This is what I remember from Lask until actually the war came in existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that they-- they struggled to make a living. What-- how did they make a living? How did they support you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a tannery, but most of the people were-- they hardly made a living, let's put it this way. We-- our family was not considered rich. I mean-- never were. You know, I know lots of people that tried to tell, you know, what they used to have at home. Naturally, when they got in concentration camp, that little bit was not even there either. So. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers or sisters? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six brothers. My father had six-- eight children-- six sons and two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came almost on the bottom, on the-- I was the youngest-- next to the youngest. Between the oldest and the youngest, that was 15 years of a spread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go down the list of-- of brothers and sisters, and tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. I know my brother, Efroim, he was the oldest. Then came my sister, Frieda. Then came my brother, Joe. Then come my brother, Lazar. Then came my brother, Leip. Then came my brother, Sam. Then I was born. And after me, my father-- he was the lucky guy-- he got a little girl after six boys, you know. And she was everything in the world for him. This, I do remember. That child was-- was his baby. We all loved her dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] In your family, how were-- how religious was your family? How were religious events?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of rituals did you practice in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't something like not kosher to come in our house. And my father was very active in the synagogue, you know. And he was a very religious individual, yeah-- very. My mother was even worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, she was strictly religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother attend synagogue, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was only one synagogue-- the Lask Synagogue. As a matter of fact, that was such a popular synagogue, that Mr. Vishniac was interested to come in 1936, to Lask, and take pictures from the synagogue, and from the rabbi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the photographer you're-- you're talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: The photographer, right-- Roman Vishniac. As a matter of fact, I got the book here. I believe I showed that to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. That was Eisenberg-- Leibele Eisenberg, they used to call him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-I-S-E-N-B-E-R-G. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned about Jewish organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the ones that either you or your family was involved with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine oldest family, my brother, he belonged to the Bund. He was very active in the Bund. This was on the-- on the left side of the-- left-- left side from the middle, let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: I personally belonged to the Betar. This was the opposite side, you know? And, listen, this is the way people used to live. I mean, we used to have organization. Each one belonged to a different organization. Your-- your parents, which way did they go? Left or-- or right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. They were religious. They didn't like what either one of us were doing. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of literature would you find in your home? What kind of things did-- did was read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we always used to have-- we always used to have the Jewish paper, the Polish paper. And the elderly people-- the people, I mean, they-- for them, the-- the-- the news was the They were also interested in what goes on in the world. Don't get me wrong. They were not fanatic. But the most important thing for them then, that was the And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk about a Jewish paper and Polish paper. What languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish exclusively?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing but Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about outside the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked Polish. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the community was about 50% Jewish. What were relations like prior to the war? What were relations like between the Jews and the non-Jews in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since Hitler came to power, in 1933, they came out. The anti-Semitism was, in Poland, very, very strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't like the Jews, either-- even before. But that was somehow-- that was-- Pilsudski had the power. I mean, he was the prime minister in Poland at that time. And somehow, that was not-- that didn't came out until, really, Hitler came to power. And they have seen what Hitler is trying to do with the Jews. And to an extent, that-- that-- they were happy to see it. They were very unfriendly to the Jewish people-- the Polish-- the Polacks, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come to the tannery? Did they give you business, or did they stay away from Jewish shops?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I mean, you had signs that shouldn't-- nobody should buy by Jewish stores. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to Hitler's rise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prior. I mean, yeah, that was-- after Hitler, that was all over. Wherever you went, I mean, you could see signs on every street. So, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was already in existence?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on --on the houses, on the-- wherever you went, I mean, you could see and [SPEAKING POLISH] I mean, that's mean that you shouldn't buy by-- by Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [SPEAKING POLISH] Don't buy by a Jew. Do not buy by Jew-- over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was what was in the windows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, that was-- that-- that-- that-- for a slogan. I mean, that the-- they should go to their own stores. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of school did you attend? Was it a Jewish school or a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. I finished public school, and then I mostly-- I went-- I went to a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you taught in the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We taught when somebody finished seven-- seven classes, for-- just like high school in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a general education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, general education, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there equal numbers of Jews and non-Jews in the classroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so, yes. The Jewish children went to separate classes. We had separate schools. Even the public schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the public school there were separate classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within the public schools, yes, they were separated. Whatever the reason was-- I know that then we had a school that was Jews and non-Jews, but we had separate classes. It was always frictions and fighting came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the separate classrooms dividing the Jews from the non-Jews, this was prior to Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- so there was anti-Semitism within the school itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure, oh sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific incidents of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, that was every-- every-- daily-- occur-- that's happened every day, you know, that-- not in particular.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like-- like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, like, that was very different today than it was two weeks ago. But I always think what's happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what? What-- what would be-- what would be an example?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could feel that you're not wanted. You're not liked. You know, that was certain streets that Jewish boys or girls were afraid to go in there, because the gentile were living in there, and they didn't welcome. We were not welcome in that street. Stones were thrown at us. And that was like, we-- we knew already in which direction, which areas we can go and be safe, and not safe-- like you got neighborhoods in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So those were the negative-- the negative aspects. What were some of the-- the things you enjoyed doing as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I enjoyed doing is to-- to ride [LAUGHS] a bike, but I couldn't have it. It was too expensive for me to have a bike. It was playing with kids like any other children, you know, like children here. And we had just as much fun. Whatever we had, we enjoyed each other, and we had fun. We were catching fish, you know, instead going to-- to-- to school, to go to Hebrew-- I mean, classes. And it's the same thing. Kids are kids wherever you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- as a child, when you thought about the future, what did you think? Did you have any-- any specific goals, anything you wanted to be or do?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I grew up, I have seen there was no future in Poland for Jewish people and for the young-- youth. We will have to run away if the occasion-- I mean, if you're going to have a chance of running. Like, for instance, people in France used to live a lot nicer and better. In Germany, before Hitler came to power, people used to live more. They had more freedom. The anti-Semitism was not in Germany as embedded like it was by-- in Poland. You see, in Germany, the anti-Semitism had to be brought out by Hitler. In Poland, there were no need for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Do you remember when you first heard about Hitler? Do remember first becoming aware of that? Or was that-- or was Hitler something-- a name that you had always heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, in 1933, I was about 10 years old. And I used to read the Jewish paper. I mean, it was very interested in what goes on in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we have seen that there is a-- a monster growing. There is a cancer growing on the Jewish nation. And this will spread-- if not the war, the anti-Semitism for sure. And it did. But we couldn't do anything, you know? Did your parents share their feelings with you about Hitler, about the Nazis? You say you-- you would read the paper. Would-- would your parents discuss it with you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe we have seen. We all-- we didn't discuss. We know. We-- we had discussions about it, but we know the end result will not-- probably will not be a good one. Because, you see, even if the war wouldn't break out, that anti-Semitism, what's happened in Germany, reflect the countries all the way around Germany. And we were-- one-- one of the brothers-- we were only, let's say, certain-- certain places, we were only 50 miles or 40 kilometers for-- you know, from-- from the German border. We knew exactly what was going on through radio and through the newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Did other Jews in your community share those feelings? Or were there split emotions? Were some people less concerned than others?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all have seen the same thing. And none of us could do anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware of-- of Hitler's rise to power, and you were following that. How did your life change specifically when the Nazis invaded your country in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in 1939, September the 1st, the war did broke out. And I remember, that was on a Friday. And people used to come, maybe about 40 kilometers away from our town, that the city was bombarded even before war was declared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people actually came without anything on them, to run away from the bombs. And we have seen, I mean, that there's a tragic thing approaching. What the end gonna be, we only know that the war started. How that's gonna end, where it's gonna end, and when it's gonna end, nobody knows. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How were-- how-- how was Hitler's-- how was the invasion at this time? You talk about how the Jews felt about it. How did the non-Jews feel about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans came into our town, the Polish population, some of them, pointed out where Jews are living. Or if we stayed in line, some of the Polacks also pointed out the Jews are in line. They-- they had to move. They had to go back-- either to the back of the line, or they chased them away from the line. I mean, they were not too friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the non-Jews being supportive in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to us. We didn't have any contact before the war with too many Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we haven't got any contact there. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: At any time, were you forced into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: At any time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you forced to live in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, on November the 11th-- that usually was a-- a holiday. Because on November 11, the Poles were liberated after 125 years being under the Russian and the German occupation. So that-- that was a day, like, we've got the 4th of July. The Germans came, and they took the-- the rabbi and the Jewish people into the marktplatz. That was a place. And they used to make a comedy show. You know, they used to beat the Jews. They had to go on. One was beating the other, you know, and running like horses around. And the rabbi, was-- they gave him a goat, and he had to slaughter the goat. I mean, they made just a-- a comedy show. And they had fun. Thanks God that we were not picked up. I remember, I was with my brother on the attic-- hiding. And we could see that they were schlepping the Jews out into the marktplatz to, you know, to make the performance. And after that, we decided-- particularly me-- I decided that I-- we have to run. If we want to save our life, we got to get out. We also knew, at that time, that Poland has been divided-- 1/2 of Poland went to the Soviets, and 1/2 of Poland was occupied by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the police station. I took a-- a piece of paper that I am-- a document that I am looking for a brother in Grodno. And Grodno wasn't under Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I took my brother with me, mine oldest brother, and another brother, Leip. But he was too-- he was-- he got sick with his leg, so we left him in Lodz. And he went back to Lask. And we went to Russia. We got into Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So when you-- you were in-- the ghetto that you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, that was no ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- this was before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: November, it was no-- no ghetto, I mean, at that time. That just-- this-- this was only a preparation, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you enter a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to Bialystok-- and I was in Bialystok until, I would say, a month or six weeks-- life was not too easy for us. But we had somebody that we know, a Mr. Tannenbaum. He was very kind and very nice to us. And my brother decided that I should go back and discuss the situation, and tell the-- my father, and what should we do next. Because we cannot be there forever. Because the Russians, what-- at that time, they're insist. They would like us that we should accept their citizenship-- Russian citizenship papers-- and ship us out to Soviet Russia. I mean, they didn't-- they-- they meant well, because there was no work, I mean. But it was hundreds of thousands of Jews escaping from the Germans right there. I mean, that was no place where to go. But I was not successful crossing the border. So I came back to Bialystok. Coming back to Bialystok, I have seen that my brother was not there anymore, and he forgot to leave the address. He probably assumed that I crossed the border and it's good that it's going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was looking for him. And I was looking for about six or seven weeks. And I was very lonely-- no place where to stay, no place-- I mean, except for the Tannenbaum family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I decided to come back. When I came back, I find my brother Efroim at home. And he took-- already organized another group of people. And he went back to Soviet Russia. And I remained in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ghetto was in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lask.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lask.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they made a ghetto. They took the worst neighborhood in the city, and they put all the people, I mean, where to live in certain areas, you know? They allocated a part-- this is the-- this is the place where Jews are gonna live. And the rest of them had to go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 19-- 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember the-- the borders of the ghetto? Do remember what streets were included?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Our street-- we used to live-- that was-- that-- that was part of the ghetto. So we had an advantage that we remained in our own house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, for the people what they used to live, let's say, in the better locations, or in a different locations, they were pushed in. I mean, that-- they lost their homes. They were only given two days. And then we had to share with them the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't even give us a break that we should take in the people what we know. They just, within two days, they had to move. So they had to pack in people wherever they could. And that was actually generated by the Germans, that it should be friction between ourselves. You see? And that-- this created frictions. Now, the-- the people that were in the ghetto now, were they just from your community of Lask? Or were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from other areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: So far, except there are-- there were certain people that they came from other towns, because they used to make judenrein certain areas not far from us. Some often came to our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What communities were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- that used to be-- belonged to Germany before the war-- I mean, before the-- the first world war, like Poznan, Bydgoszcz. And also, a lots of ghettos that feel they had family in Lask, they came. A lots of people came to our ghettos, all right, by themself-- not forced in, but they came voluntarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: People voluntarily came to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they came because the-- their cities had to be wiped out of Jews, you know-- so ended up going someplace. They used to ship 'em to Lublin, in the vicinity-- so ended up going there. But if they have families, relative in our town, they came to Lask.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said some of the communities that came to-- to-- to the-- to the ghetto in Lask were Poznan, and what was the other community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poznan, Bydgoszcz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell those for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poznan is P-A-- oh-- P-O-S-N-A-N-- Poznan. That was a big-- big city in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other two communities you mentioned, could you spell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Kalisz-- K-A-L-I-S-S-- or S-Z. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other community?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I mean, those two places--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is what they're-- actually, they cleaned them out. They did want to have judenrein. Right to 1939, in a way, they were better off, because there they had a chance to escape to Soviet Russia. And the remainder, because there were no way to place to go for them back. When I was in Soviet Russia, I-- I was-- I was missing my home. For the people where they were liquidated, I mean, where they made judenrein, clear of Jews, they haven't got a home to go where. I wish I wouldn't have a home to go back, and I would remain in Soviet Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me a little bit about the ghetto. Was there a wall around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an open ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Open.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody was watching us. And nobody was running away. If they caught you, after some-- if they catch somebody out of the ghetto, they were shot. That wasn't no Polish police and no German police for-- for to guard us. If we had to get out, we could only walk from 8 o'clock in the morning. That was a curfew to about 6:00 or 7:00. That all depends.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to remain in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to remain. We had to remain in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were conditions like in the ghetto? What were sanitary conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. For most of the people, what they came to our neighborhood, it was very, extremely bad. We had to make kitchens. I know lots of people went to public kitchens, you know, that was organized by the Jews and also the Germans. The food came from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: We tried to go also in the evening for people that did not need to eat in the public places, to make 'em feel at home, you know-- that everybody joined in, you know? Because it was very respectful people. I mean, within-- within three, four months, they had nothing. They had to leave their apartment with-- with-- within two hours, sometime a day. So how many people-- well, you already had a large family. How many people were in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was another family. We used to have a room, a kitchen, and a bedroom. They put in another family to us. And there were five people in the other family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people were living in how many rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were living in lots of people. I mean, we always used to sleep, you know, during the ghetto, at four, five people in one bed. I mean, this-- this was a normal thing. I mean, we had to take those people in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If we had something more, we used to share with people what didn't have. My mother used to take care on those thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talk about getting the food from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. Boy, that went fast again.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was 2 and 1/2 when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tapped you.
